elements.sent-2411-001: The abstract should briefly state the purpose of the manuscript, the problem to be addressed, the approach taken, and the nature of results or conclusions that can be expected.
elements.sent-2411-002: It should stand independently and tell enough about the manuscript to permit the reader to decide whether the subject is of specific interest.
elements.sent-2411-003: The abstract shall be typed single space, justified, centered, and with a column width of 4.5 inches.
elements.sent-2411-004: The abstract is not preceded by a heading of ``Abstract'' and its length may not extend beyond the first page.
elements.sent-2411-005: The American Astronautical Society (AAS) publishes bound sets of printed conference proceedings for personal, institutional, and library usage.
elements.sent-2411-006: The availability of hardcopy enhances the longevity of your work and elevates the importance of your conference contribution to professionals through archival publication.
elements.sent-2411-007: To preserve the consistency and quality of the proceedings, all authors adhere to the latest version of AAS conference proceedings format.
elements.sent-2411-008: This document is intended to serve as a visual and instructional guide, and as a \LaTeX\ document template, for the AAS conference proceedings format.
elements.sent-2411-009: This template provides the basic font sizes, styles, and margins required by the publisher's formatting instructions.
elements.sent-2411-010: There are also styles for centered equations, figure and table captions, section and sub-section headings, footnote text, etc. This template provides samples of their usage.
elements.sent-2411-011: To use this document as a template, simply copy and change its contents with your own information while maintaining the required predefined style, rather than starting anew.
elements.sent-2411-012: Since this is not a tutorial on how to use \LaTeX\, refer to \LaTeX\ manuals for more information.
elements.sent-2411-013: Your manuscript should include a paper number, a title, an author listing, an abstract, an introductory section, one or more sections containing the main body of the manuscript, a concluding summary section, and a reference or bibliography section.
elements.sent-2411-014: You may also include a section on notation, an acknowledgements section, and appendices, as illustrated in the sequel.
elements.sent-2411-015: You should not include a leading cover sheet.
elements.sent-2411-016: Author affiliation shall appear on the first page, added as a footnote to the last name of each the author.
elements.sent-2411-017: If a distributional release statement or copyright notice is required by your sponsor, this is added as a footnote to the title of the manuscript, appearing on the first page only.
elements.sent-2411-018: Page numbers should be centered halfway between the lower margin and the bottom edge of the page (i.e., approximately 0.75 inches from the bottom).
elements.sent-2411-019: Copy should be single space with double space between paragraphs, with the first line of each paragraph indented 0.2 inches.
elements.sent-2411-020: The recommended sans-serif font for paper number, title, and author listing is Arial, or, Helvetica.
elements.sent-2411-021: The title font and paper-number font should be the same: 14-point sans-serif, centered, and bold.
elements.sent-2411-022: The author-listing font should be 12-point sans-serif, centered, and bold.
elements.sent-2411-023: The recommended serif font for body text, headings, etc., is Times or Times New Roman at 10-12 point, 11 point preferred.
elements.sent-2411-024: The captions for figures and tables are bold 10-point serif font.
elements.sent-2411-025: The endnote reference text and footnote text is 9-point serif font.
elements.sent-2411-026: The right-hand margin of body text should be justified; if not, it should be fairly even nevertheless.
elements.sent-2411-027: All text and text background shall remain uncolored (black on white).
elements.sent-2411-028: These conventions should be automatically implemented in this \LaTeX\ template when the predefined styles of this template are used.
elements.sent-2411-029: The body text of this template is based on the preferred font size of 11 points.
elements.sent-2411-030: To change this to 12-point size, increase the font size at the top of the \LaTeX\ template by uncommenting the appropriate {\tt documentclass[]\{\} line.}
elements.sent-2411-031: For very long manuscripts, a 10-point font may be used to keep the manuscript within the publisher's limit of twenty (20) physical pages.
elements.section-2411-002: This is a Sample of a General Section Heading
elements.sent-2411-032: Numbering of section headings and paragraphs should be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-033: Major section headings are majuscule, bold, flush (aligned) left, and use the same style san-serif font as the body text.
elements.sent-2411-034: Widow and orphan lines should also be avoided; more than one line of a paragraph should appear at the end or beginning of a page, not one line by itself.
elements.sent-2411-035: A heading should not appear at the bottom of a page without at least two lines of text.
elements.sent-2411-036: Equations, figures, and tables must be sequentially numbered with no repeated numbers or gaps.
elements.sent-2411-037: Excessive white space --- such as large gaps before, between, and after text and figures --- should be minimal and eliminated where possible.
elements.subsection-2411-001: This Is a Sample of a Secondary (Sub-Section) Heading
elements.sent-2411-038: Secondary, or sub-section, headings are title case (miniscule lettering with the first letter of major words majuscule), flush left, and bold.
elements.sent-2411-039: Secondary headings use the same serif font style as the body text and, like section headings, should not be numbered.
elements.sent-2411-040: Tertiary headings should be avoided, but if necessary, they are run-in, italic, and end in a period, as illustrated with the next six (6) paragraphs. \begin{equation}\label{eq:ab} a = b^{2}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: Equations are centered with the equation number flush to the right.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the text, these equations should be referenced by name as Eq.~\eqref{eq:ab or Equation~\eqref{eq:ab (e.g., not Eq. 1, (1), or Equation 1).}}
elements.sent-2411-043: To improve readability, scalar variable names such as $a$ and $b^{2}$ are usually italicized when appearing in text and equations.\footnote{A section on mathematical notation is provided in the sequel.}
elements.sent-2411-044: When abbreviations for units of measure are used, lower case without periods is preferred in most instances; e.g. ft, yd, sec, ft/sec, etc., but in.. for inch.
elements.sent-2411-045: Illustrations are referenced by name and without formatting embellishments, such as Figure~\ref{fig:xxx , Figure 2, etc., or, Figures 3 and 4 (e.g., not figure (1), Fig. 1, Figure 1, Figure 1, etc.).}
elements.sent-2411-046: Each illustration should have a caption unless it is a mere sketch.
elements.sent-2411-047: Single-phrase captions are usually in title case; they are bold 10-point serif font and centered below the figure as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:xxx .}
elements.sent-2411-048: An explanatory caption of several sentences is permissible.
elements.sent-2411-049: Ideally, every illustration should be legibly sized -- usually about one-half or one-quarter page -- and appear in the text just before it is called out or mentioned.
elements.sent-2411-050: Alternatively, it is also permissible to place all figures together at the end of the text as a separate appendix; however, these two conventions should not be mixed.
elements.sent-2411-051: All figures and callouts should remain clearly legible after reduction.
elements.sent-2411-052: All illustrations appear as black and white in the final printing, although colors are retained in the electronic (CD-ROM) version.
elements.sent-2411-053: The highest quality formats are Encapsulated PostScript (EPS) and PDF vector-graphic formats.
elements.sent-2411-054: These formats are recommended for all illustrations, unless they create document files that are excessively large.
elements.sent-2411-055: Specifically, you should change the graphic format or compress the image resolution whenever an illustrated page takes more than two seconds to render onscreen, or, whenever the total manuscript file size starts to approach 5 Mb.
elements.sent-2411-056: Photographs, illustrations that use heavy toner or ink (such as bar graphs), and figures without text callouts, may be suitably displayed with picture formats such as BMP, GIF, JPEG, PNG, TIFF, etc. Line drawings, plots, and callouts on illustrations, should not use picture formats that do not provide sharp reproduction.
elements.sent-2411-057: All graphical content must be embedded when creating a PDF document, especially any fonts used within the illustration.
elements.sent-2411-058: Note that the Windows Metafile Format (WMF) is sometimes problematic and should be avoided..
elements.sent-2411-059: The citation of bibliographical endnote references is indicated in the text by superscripted Arabic numerals, preferably at the end of a sentence.\cite{doe2005, style1959 If this citation causes confusion in mathematics, or if a superscript is inappropriate for other reasons, this may be alternately expressed as (Reference~\citenum{doe2005 ) or (see References~\citenum{doe2005 and \citenum{style1959 ), (e.g., not [1], Ref. (1), etc.).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: While there is no singly prescribed format for every bibliographical endnote, references should be consistent in form.
elements.sent-2411-061: Citations should be sufficient to allow the reader to precisely find the information being cited, and should include specific pages, editions, and printing numbers where necessary.
elements.sent-2411-062: URL citations are discouraged, especially when an archival source for the same information is available.
elements.sent-2411-063: If a URL citation is required, it should appear completely and as a footnote instead of a bibliographical reference.\footnote{\url{http://www.univelt.com/FAQ.html\#SUBMISSION The citation of private communication is especially discouraged, but if required it should be cited as a footnote and include the date, professional affiliation, and location of the person cited.\footnote{Gangster, Maurice (1999), personal correspondence of March 21st.. Sr.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Consultant, Space Cowboy Associates, Inc., Colorado Springs, CO.
elements.figure-2411-002: \fontsize{10}{10}\selectfont \caption{A Caption Goes Here} \label{tab:label} \begin{tabular}{c | r | r } Animal Description Price (\$) Gnat per gram 13.65 each 0.01 Gnu stuffed 92.50 Emu stuffed 33.33 Armadillo frozen 8.99 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-065: Tables are referred to by name in the text as Table~\ref{tab:label , or, Tables 2 and 3 (e.g., not table 1, Tbl.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The title is centered above the table, as shown in Table 1.
elements.sent-2411-067: The font size inside tables should be no larger than the body text, but may be adjusted down to 9-point if necessary (10-point serif font is considered nominal).
elements.sent-2411-068: Note that table units are in parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-069: Only the minimum number of table lines needed for clarity is desired.
elements.sent-2411-070: Ideally, every table should appear within the text just before it is called out, but, it is also permissible to place all tables together at the end of the text as a separate appendix.
elements.sent-2411-071: If so, these two conventions should not be mixed.
elements.sent-2411-072: Each figure and table shall be called out in the text; gratuitous figures and tables that are not called out should be eliminated.
elements.sent-2411-073: Intermediate equations may be numbered without being called out.
elements.sent-2411-074: The Portable Document Format (PDF) is the preferred format for electronic submissions.\footnote{By contributing your manuscript for proceedings publication, you necessarily extend any copyrights to the AAS and its designated publisher, to allow the AAS to publish your manuscript content in all the forms that it wishes.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The page size should be 8.5 inches by 11 inches exactly.
elements.sent-2411-076: You should use ``press-quality'' or ``high-quality'' software settings to create your PDF file; these settings tend to keep the PDF file true to the original manuscript layout, and automatically embed the correct fonts, etc. Otherwise, settings such as ``Embed All Fonts'', etc., should be selected as available.
elements.sent-2411-077: The use of internal hyperlinks within the electronic file is not encouraged because hyperlinks may not be supported in the final version of the electronic proceedings.
elements.sent-2411-078: If you wish to submit this manuscript to the Journal of Astronautical Sciences, it must be re-formatted into a double-spaced format.
elements.sent-2411-079: This can be done easily with this template.
elements.sent-2411-080: At the top of the document, there are two (2) types document class statements ({\tt paper and {\tt submit ).}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The first type is the one to use for a conference paper.
elements.sent-2411-082: The second type , which is commented out, can be used to reformat the paper for the JAS journal submission.
elements.sent-2411-083: Some AAS meetings are co-sponsored with the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics (AIAA).
elements.sent-2411-084: When your paper number starts with ``AAS'', or when the conference is described as a joint ``AAS/AIAA'' meeting with the AAS listed first, this AAS conference proceedings format shall be used.
elements.sent-2411-085: Your final manuscript should be camera-ready as submitted --- free from technical, typographical, and formatting errors.
elements.sent-2411-086: Manuscripts not suitable for publication are omitted from the final proceedings.
elements.sent-2411-087: Any acknowledgments by the author may appear here.
elements.sent-2411-089: Where the first mathematical symbol is introduced, a footnote should direct the attention of the reader to this table.\footnote{The footnote symbols are a standard sequence: $\ast$, $\dagger$, $\ddag$, etc. This sequence of footnote symbols should restart with each new page.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The notation table should be simple and reasonably consistent with the standards of modern technical journals, as illustrated above.
elements.sent-2411-091: The notation table does not need its own caption like an ordinary table, since the section heading serves this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-093: Each appendix is its own section with its own section heading.
elements.sent-2411-094: The title of each appendix section is preceded by ``APPENDIX: '' as illustrated above, or ``APPENDIX A: '', ``APPENDIX B: '', etc., when multiple appendixes are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-095: Appendices are optional and normally go after references; however, appendices may go ahead of the references section whenever the word processor forces superscripted endnotes to the very end of the document.
elements.sent-2411-096: The contents of each appendix must be called out at least once in the body of the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-097: The page size shall be the American standard of 8.5 inches by 11 inches (216 mm x 279 mm).
elements.sent-2411-098: Margins are as follows: Top -- 0.75 inch (19 mm); Bottom -- 1.5 inches (38 mm); Left -- 1.25 inches (32 mm); Right -- 1.25 inch (32 mm).
elements.sent-2411-099: The title of the manuscript starts one inch (25.4 mm) below the top margin.
elements.sent-2411-100: Column width is 6 inches (152.5 mm) and column length is 8.75 inches (222.5 mm).
elements.sent-2411-101: The abstract is 4.5 inches (114 mm) in width, centered, justified, 10 point normal (serif) font.. \bibliographystyle{AAS_publication.}
elements.sent-2411-102: In this work, we investigate trajectories that require thrust to maintain periodic structure in the circular restricted three-body problem (CR3BP).
elements.sent-2411-103: We produce bounds in position and velocity space for the energy-constrained reachable set of initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our trajectories are energy-optimal and analyzed via linear analysis.
elements.sent-2411-105: We provide validation for our technique and analyze the cost of deviating in various directions to the reference.
elements.sent-2411-106: For our given reference, we find that it is relatively expensive to decrease perilune distance for orbits in the Earth-Moon system.
elements.sent-2411-107: Uncontrolled, periodic orbits in the circular restricted three-body problem (CR3BP) have been well-studied and are often exploited in spacecraft operations.
elements.sent-2411-108: There exist families of these periodic orbits centered around the five equilibrium Lagrange points in a three-body system.
elements.sent-2411-109: Of particular interest are periodic orbits around the L1 and L2 points, as these orbits have historically been used by spacecraft in the Earth-Sun system and are planned for use in the Earth-Moon system.
elements.sent-2411-110: A constant perturbation applied to the CR3BP (e.g., a constant acceleration provided by low thrust propulsion), will shift the equilibrium points and allow areas beyond the natural space to be exploited \cite{Cox2019, Cox2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Because the equilibrium points have been shifted, the periodic trajectories around the equilibria will be shifted as well \cite{Morimoto2006, Tsuruta2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: Many previous studies have primarily focused on constant, low thrust trajectories \cite{Cox2019, Cox2020, Morimoto2006, DeLeo2022 or optimal control in proximity of the shifted equilibria \cite{Tsuruta2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Other related work exists that focuses on forced circumnavigation or controlled loitering trajectories relative to a spacecraft on some reference orbit in the CR3BP \cite{Sandel2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: In this work, we also investigate optimally controlled trajectories relative to a reference orbit.
elements.sent-2411-115: However, the emphasis here is on developing and characterizing a catalog of forced periodic trajectories (trajectories that begin and end at the same state) under control rather than on the relative motion of two real satellites.
elements.sent-2411-116: Additionally, the lens through which we examine optimally controlled trajectories in the vicinity of a reference orbit leverages the analytical aspects of techniques from relative reachable set theory to provide a lower fidelity but more holistic view of optimally controlled trajectories near a reference orbit.
elements.sent-2411-117: We refer to these orbits as forced periodic trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-118: While not periodic in the natural dynamics, these trajectories expand available options for satellite orbits in the CR3BP beyond naturally periodic or quasi-periodic structures.
elements.sent-2411-119: As an initial investigation into this problem, we make use of the linearized model of energy-optimal control and approximate the energy cost of optimal forced periodic trajectories near a naturally periodic orbit about the L2 point in the Earth-Moon system.
elements.sent-2411-120: This periodic orbit will serve as a reference trajectory for our spacecraft and we will work to understand how small perturbations in the initial and final state from this reference trajectory affect the cost of control.
elements.sent-2411-121: Here, we are interested in finding the reachable set of states for our system in the energy-constrained problem.
elements.sent-2411-122: This formalism is based on the work in Kulik et al., 2024 \cite{Kulik2024 , where it is expanded upon further.}
elements.sent-2411-123: We specialize results from this work to the context of forced periodic trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-124: We assume a dynamical system of the form \begin{equation}\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}}{\mathrm{d}t}=\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})+\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{0}_{3\times1} \mathbf{u} \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} where the state vector $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R ^6$ is defined by stacking the position and velocity vectors $\mathbf{x =[\mathbf{r ^T, \mathbf{v ^T]^T$ and $\mathbf{u}$ is the control acceleration vector.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: In our system, $\mathbf{F (\mathbf{x )$ gives the natural dynamics for the third body in the ``canonical rotating frame" of the CR3BP \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} v_{x} v_{y} v_{z} 2v_{y} + x - (1-\mu^*)\frac{x+\mu^*}{R_1^3} - \mu^*\frac{x-1+\mu^*}{R_2^3} -2v_{x} + y - (1-\mu^*)\frac{y}{R_1^3} - \mu^*\frac{y}{R_2^3} -(1-\mu^*)\frac{z}{R_1^3} - \mu^*\frac{z}{R_2^3} \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} where $x$, $y$, and $z$ are the components of the spacecraft's position vector, $\mathbf{r}$, and $v_x$, $v_y$, and $v_z$ are the components of the spacecraft's velocity vector, $\mathbf{v}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: The distances of the third body with respect to the primary and secondary are defined as $R_1$ and $R_2$, respectively, and are evaluated as \begin{equation}R_1 = \sqrt{(x+\mu^*)^2 + y^2 + z^2} R_2 = \sqrt{(x-1+\mu^*)^2 + y^2 + z^2}\end{equation} where $\mu^* = m_2/(m_1+m_2)$ is the mass parameter of the system.
elements.sent-2411-127: Defining a quadratic cost function of the form \begin{equation}J = \frac{1}{2}\int_{t_0}^{t_f} ||\mathbf{u}||^2 dt\end{equation} the optimal control from one state to another is given by solving a two-point boundary value problem associated with a system of ordinary differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-128:
These equations have twice as many dimensions as the state of the original system and are given by \begin{equation}\frac{dx}{dt} =F(x)+\begin{bmatrix} 0_{3\times1}\end{equation}
\begin{equation}u\end{bmatrix} \frac{d\lambda}{dt} =-\left(\frac{\partial F(x)}{\partial x}\right)^T \lambda u = -\lambda_v\end{equation} where $\bm{\lambda_v}$ is the velocity costate vector given by the last three elements of the costate vector \cite{bryson2018applied .}
elements.sent-2411-129: The state transition matrix (STM) associated with the augmented state vector and its dynamics yields a linear approximation of perturbations to the final augmented state $\delta\mathbf{y (t_f)$ at some final time, $t_f$, as a function of deviations in the initial augmented state: \begin{equation}\delta\mathbf{y}(t_f) = \begin{bmatrix} \delta\mathbf{r}(t_f) \delta\mathbf{v}(t_f) \delta\bm{\lambda}_r(t_f) \delta\bm{\lambda_v}(t_f) \end{bmatrix} \approx \STMd{}{}(t_f,t_0) \delta\mathbf{y}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} \STMa{x}{x} \STMb{x}{\lambda} \STMc{\lambda}{x} \STMd{\lambda}{\lambda} \end{bmatrix} \delta\mathbf{y}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} \STMa{r}{r} \STMa{r}{v} \STMb{r}{\lambda_r} \STMb{r}{\lambda_v} \STMa{v}{r} \STMa{v}{v} \STMb{v}{\lambda_r} \STMb{v}{\lambda_v} \STMc{\lambda_r}{r} \STMc{\lambda_r}{v} \STMd{\lambda_r}{\lambda_r} \STMd{\lambda_r}{\lambda_v} \STMc{\lambda_v}{r} \STMc{\lambda_v}{v} \STMd{\lambda_v}{\lambda_r} \STMd{\lambda_v}{\lambda_v} \end{bmatrix} \delta\mathbf{y}_0\end{equation} where $\delta\mathbf{y _0 = \delta\mathbf{y (t_0)$ is the perturbed initial state and $\bm{\Phi (t_f,t_0)$ is a time-varying STM associated with the augmented state, reference trajectory, and initial and final times.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: We adopt the notation \begin{equation}\STMa{b}{a}(t,t_0) = \frac{\partial \mathbf{b}(t)}{\partial \mathbf{a}(t_0)}\end{equation} If the time dependence of an STM is omitted in this paper, it indicates that the STM corresponds to a full period (i.e., $\STMd{ { (t_f,t_0) = \STMd{ {}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Substituting in to the cost function, we now have \begin{equation}J = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} \delta\mathbf{x}_0 \delta\mathbf{x}_f \end{bmatrix}^T \mathbf{E} \begin{bmatrix} \delta\mathbf{x}_0 \delta\mathbf{x}_f \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} Relative reachable set theory considers the set of $\delta\mathbf{x _f$ that can be reached given some fixed starting condition $\delta\mathbf{x _0$ or some set of potential starting points $\delta\mathbf{x _0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Much of this energy-limited reachable set theory is accomplished using ellipsoidal geometry associated with the quadratic form described by the matrix $\mathbf{E}$ and the eigenvalue decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-133: We will use these same techniques as well as extensions to generalized eigenvalues and eigenvectors to study the set of forced periodic trajectories that satisfy an energy constraint.
elements.sent-2411-134: Forced periodic trajectories in the vicinity of a periodic reference orbit satisfy the condition $\delta\mathbf{x _f=\delta\mathbf{x _0$, so that the final state is equivalent to the initial state after one period of the reference periodic orbit.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: To study the set of forced periodic trajectories that require less than some energy limit, we may study the following matrix \begin{equation}\mathbf{E}^* = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_6 \mathbf{I}_6 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{E} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_6 \mathbf{I}_6 \end{bmatrix}^T\end{equation} so that the linearized cost function for the forced periodic trajectory beginning and ending at $\delta\mathbf{x _0$ can then be written as \begin{equation}J = \frac{1}{2}\delta\mathbf{x}_0^T \mathbf{E}^* \delta\mathbf{x}_0\end{equation} Now assume that ($\gamma_i, \mathbf{w _i$) is an eigenpair of the matrix $\mathbf{E ^*$ where $\gamma_i$ is some eigenvalue and $\mathbf{w _i$ is its corresponding eigenvector.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Since $\mathbf{E ^*$ is a symmetric positive semi-definite matrix, the set of possible relative states $\delta\mathbf{x _0$ that cost less than some energy limit $J^*$ to begin and end at under linearized optimal control is given by the hyperellipsoid described by the set \begin{equation}\left\{ \delta\mathbf{x}_0 \quad \mathrm{s.t.}\quad \frac{1}{2}\delta\mathbf{x}_0^T \mathbf{E}^* \delta\mathbf{x}_0 \leq J^* \right\}\end{equation} whose semi-axes are given by the vectors \begin{equation}\mathbf{a}_i=\sqrt{\frac{2J^*}{\gamma_i}}\mathbf{w}_i \label{eq:semiaxes}\end{equation} Thus, we have defined the set of relative states which can be returned to in a period of the reference orbit with cost no greater than $J^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: The ellipsoid described here is in 6-dimensional position and velocity space.
elements.sent-2411-138: Our initial investigation involves selecting a reference halo orbit around L2.
elements.sent-2411-139: Because the reference orbit is naturally periodic, the initial phasing may be chosen at will.
elements.sent-2411-140: Here, we select the initial condition near apolune.
elements.sent-2411-141: The initial conditions for our chosen orbit are \begin{equation}\begin{bmatrix} x y z v_x v_y v_z \bm{\lambda} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.06315768 \:\: \text{DU} 0.000326952322 \:\: \text{DU} -0.200259761 \:\: \text{DU} 0.000361619362 \:\: \text{DU/TU} -0.176727245 \:\: \text{DU/TU} -0.000739327422 \:\: \text{DU/TU} \mathbf{0}_{6\times1} \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} with a period of 2.085034838884136 TU and a mass constant of 0.01215059.
elements.sent-2411-142: Propagation of our states and STMs is performed numerically in canonical units with an explicit 8th order Runge-Kutta integrator with absolute and relative error tolerances set to 1e-13.
elements.sent-2411-143: All computation is handled through precomputed STMs for the reference trajectory, as used in previous studies \cite{Kulik2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: In order to validate our method, we use a Newton-Raphson iteration scheme to find the true energy-optimal cost of our target orbits.
elements.sent-2411-145: We first determine the ``inherent cost'' to maintain the periodic reference orbits that we select.
elements.sent-2411-146: Although in theory these naturally periodic orbits should require no cost to maintain, there is typically some inherent numerical error in the initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-147: Thus, the ``true'' cost includes error from the imperfect initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-148: This is important for the validation analysis because the cost found by the Newton-Raphson scheme is inaccurate for cases where the cost would be lower or near the orbit's inherent cost.
elements.sent-2411-149: Therefore, we only consider the costs of orbits that are at least an order of magnitude greater than this inherent cost.
elements.sent-2411-150: Our reference trajectory's inherent cost is $\approx$ 3.5e-14 DU$^2$/TU$^3$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Absolute_Error.pdf}\label{fig:f1}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Linear_Computed_Cost.pdf}\label{fig:f2}} \caption{The left panel shows the difference between the estimated and computed costs while the right panel shows the estimated and computed costs. These deviations are made in the direction of the 4th eigenvector in Table \ref{tab:eigen}.} \label{fig:costs}
elements.sent-2411-151: In Figure \ref{fig:costs , we see that the cost to maintain a periodic orbit follows a quadratic form and that the linear analysis provides a good approximation of the full nonlinear analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-152: In the range of Figure \ref{fig:costs , the error is at least 3 orders of magnitude below the true cost value (corresponding to $<$ 0.1\% error).}
elements.sent-2411-153: The error grows as $\delta \mathbf{x _0$ increases, as expected in this analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-154: For deviations less than that shown in Figure \ref{fig:costs , the linear analysis should be a good approximation of the true cost, although the inherent cost does begin to dominate the error.}
elements.sent-2411-155: We now introduce a thrust constraint to analyze the extents of the energy-limited reachable set.
elements.sent-2411-156: For a given spacecraft with maximum acceleration from thrust $u_\text{max}$ and with the assumption that the spacecraft will thrust for the entire orbital period, we can find \begin{equation}J^* = \frac{1}{2} u_{\text{max}}^2 (t_f-t_0)\end{equation} where $t_f-t_0$ is the time required to complete a single period.
elements.sent-2411-157: As it is written, $J^*$ may be understood as the maximum specific power expended during a single orbit.
elements.sent-2411-158: With this constraint, we can solve for the extents of the semiaxes of the energy-limited reachable set.
elements.sent-2411-159: For a base design, let us assume a representative spacecraft with an initial mass of 1000 kg and thrust of 50 mN, translating to $u_\text{max}$ = 5E-5 m/s$^2 \: \approx$ 0.0184 DU/TU$^2$ and $J^*$ = 3.51E-4 DU$^2$/TU$^3$.
elements.sent-2411-160: This cost constraint is selected, in particular, because it falls in the range of costs with $<$ 1\% error and is a currently achievable thrust level for a propulsion system.
elements.sent-2411-161: Thus, it makes for a good example of this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-162: Using the thrust constraint paired with the linear analysis, we can study the reachable set.
elements.sent-2411-163: The reachable set takes the shape of a 6-dimensional hyper-ellipsoid with extents and semi-axes directions specified by the thrust constraint and eigenstructure of $\mathbf{E ^*$, as listed in Table \ref{tab:eigen .}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The directions of the semi-axes are given by the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{E ^*$ and the extents of the semi-axes can be found by taking the magnitude of Equation \ref{eq:semiaxes .}}
elements.sent-2411-165: The eigenvector associated with the smallest eigenvalue corresponds to the least expensive direction to deviate from the reference trajectory, which presents itself as the largest semi-axis extent.
elements.sent-2411-166: In this case, the largest extent should be infinite, as it corresponds to the zero eigenvalue.
elements.sent-2411-167: However, there is numerical error and so the first eigenvalue has a finite length here.
elements.sent-2411-168: For this reason, we do not present results that correspond to deviations in the direction of the first eigenvector.
elements.figure-2411-005: \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{YellowBounds_XY.png}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{YellowBounds_XZ.png}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{YellowBounds_YZ.png}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth,clip=true, trim=50pt 0pt 20pt 50pt]{3d_trajectories.png}} \caption{The reachable sets in position space relative to the reference trajectory via sampling. The initial conditions are sampled 100,000 times and propagated for a period to produce these orbits. The direction of the initial deviation is randomized but the cost for each is $J^*$ = 3.51E-4 DU$^2$/TU$^3$.} \label{fig:Trajectories}
elements.figure-2411-006: \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Eigen_dX.pdf}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Eigen_dVX.pdf}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Eigen_dY.pdf}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Eigen_dVY.pdf}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Eigen_dZ.pdf}} \subfloat{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Eigen_dVZ.pdf}} \caption{The difference between the reference trajectory (shown in solid black for all plots) and deviations in the final five eigenvectors. Deviations in the direction of the first eigenvector are not shown, as the extent is theoretically infinite and corresponds to the along-track direction of the orbit.} \label{fig:eigen}
elements.sent-2411-169: Figure \ref{fig:Trajectories shows the physical extents in position space of the trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-170: We sample from the boundaries of the six-dimensional reachable set 100,000 times and solve the linearized boundary value problem for each set of initial deviations to obtain initial costates.
elements.sent-2411-171: We then linearly propagate the augmented state deviations and costates and plot all the resulting trajectories in some lower-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-172: Due to the constraints of sampling, the reachable bounds in Figure \ref{fig:Trajectories are not solid and display some gaps and asymmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-173: These are products of incomplete sampling and the true reachable set does not display these phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-174: In Figure \ref{fig:Trajectories , the greatest deviation in the $x$ direction is found at apolune, where the orbit is initialized.}
elements.sent-2411-175: This can also be seen in Figure \ref{fig:eigen , where deviations in the $x$ and $z$ directions are maximized when at apolune.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Likewise, the smallest deviations are found at perilune, our half-period.
elements.sent-2411-177: The size of the bounded region is a function of $J^*$, as increasing the cost will expand the reachable set.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{Eigen_Thrust.pdf} \caption{The thrusting magnitude over a single orbit for deviations in the final five eigenvectors.} \label{fig:thrust}
elements.sent-2411-178: Notice in Figure \ref{fig:eigen that the orbits begin and conclude at the same point, as expected of periodic trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-179: However, the costates and thus the thrust do not necessarily begin and end at the same values.
elements.sent-2411-180: Thus, the augmented state is not necessarily periodic but the state that strictly includes the position and velocity vectors is periodic.
elements.sent-2411-181: Figure \ref{fig:eigen is particularly insightful in how deviations in the initial condition can cascade through the orbit.}
elements.sent-2411-182: For example, assume we wish to decrease perilune distance for our spacecraft in order to obtain better images of the Lunar surface.
elements.sent-2411-183: In order to achieve this, the spacecraft should deviate such that $\delta x < 0$, $\delta z < 0$, and $\delta y = 0$ at perilune.
elements.sent-2411-184: From inspection of Figure \ref{fig:eigen , deviations along the direction of the fifth eigenvector will achieve this, as it fulfills these three qualities.}
elements.sent-2411-185: The fifth eigenvector corresponds to the second most-expensive direction to deviate from the reference, so this also indicates that reduction in perilune distance is relatively expensive.
elements.sent-2411-186: With this analysis, we have specified bounds on forced periodic trajectories in the CR3BP.
elements.sent-2411-187: These structures are likely to provide unique observation and operational opportunities for satellites.
elements.sent-2411-188: We analyzed these forced periodic trajectories via linear analysis and we determine that for the energy-optimal case, the full augmented state is not periodic but the state that only includes position and velocity is periodic.
elements.sent-2411-189: The linear analysis also allows us to determine the least and most costly directions that deviations to the state may be made.
elements.sent-2411-190: In particular, deviations along the fifth eigenvector listed in Table \ref{tab:eigen , corresponding to the second-largest eigenvalue, will reduce the perilune distance of a spacecraft for our reference orbit.}
